Data.MeaningHint: By writing up new articles and laws they keep taking things away from samurai. First the katana, now jackhammers?
Data.ReadingHint: Genghis Khan knew that samurai were a threat to his kingdom, so he took away their secondary weapon, the jackhammer.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: The one who wrote these <kanji>article</kanji>s with such <kanji>sincerity</kanji> was Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん).
Data.MeaningMnemonic: "For the  <radical>samurai</radical>, it is a <radical>jackhammer</radical> that they will <radical>lack</radical>." That is the latest <kanji>article</kanji> of the law written about samurai, hundreds of years after katana were taken away. This article was written with great <kanji>sincerity</kanji> by the government.

Data.MeaningHint: They're hard to see because a tsunami is pretty big, but your scooter and car do create ripples in the water as it goes past.
Data.ReadingHint: As you watch the ripples come off your car and scooter, be sure to imagine the relief you'd feel if you knew they were rentals and not something owned by you.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>Ripples</kanji> come off of your scooter and car from when they were hit by a tsunami. More importantly, those things were <reading>ren</reading>tals (れん), so ripples or not, it doesn't matter if they were hit by a tsunami.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: As the <radical>tsunami</radical> hits your <radical>scooter</radical> and your <radical>car</radical>, it alters the wave, creating <kanji>ripples</kanji>.

Data.MeaningHint: Picture someone else riding a scooter with an axe in their fingertips. That's so dangerous! One bump, one bad turn, and that axe slips and they'll die.
Data.ReadingHint: Your holy blue saber died for you. It saved you from certain death and now it died. After all you've been through together. All 60 levels. This is the end.
Data.ReadingMnemonic:
You slip. The axe collides with your face. You <kanji>die</kanji>. But... wait! There's a bright, blue glow and suddenly you're un-dead. You watch in slow motion as your holy blue <reading>sa</reading>ber (せい) brings you back to life and takes the hit from the axe instead.

Wait, no!

It shatters.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: If you ride a <radical>scooter</radical> with your <radical>fingers</radical> around an <radical>axe</radical> you're going to <kanji>die</kanji>. I mean, probably. There's a 99% chance you'll die.

Data.MeaningHint: You don't really want to be in this cage, but there were some things you didn't want to do today, so you hid. Luckily these flowers block the right side of the cage and hide you completely.
Data.ReadingHint: "N-no?" you respond. "Wrong! I hid all the time. There's nothing wrong with hiding, you're doing a great job." he says, and disappears.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: As you <kanji>hide</kanji> the ghost of <reading>Toku</reading>gawa (とく) Ieyasu appears before you, inside the cage! His arms are crossed and he's shaking his head at you. "Do you think I managed to become the first shougun of Japan by hiding?"
Data.MeaningMnemonic: This <radical>cage</radical> is full of <radical>flowers</radical> on the <radical>right</radical> side to <kanji>hide</kanji> the fact that you're in here too.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine the geoduck laughing and screaming, "Now you're a widow! Now you're a widow!" flailing the sword around. It's awful.
Data.ReadingHint: It's just you and your car now, living the widow life, driving on into the sunset and forgetting your past.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: You may be a <kanji>widow</kanji>, but that doesn't make you weak. You run outside, jump into your <reading>ca</reading>r (か), and run your entire house and the geoduck over.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Under your own <radical>roof</radical>, a <radical>geoduck</radical> jumps out of the <radical>ground</radical> with a <radical>sword</radical> and stabs your husband/wife/marriage partner to death. Now you're a <kanji>widow</kanji>.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine back to the last time you ate something bad and it gave you diarrhea. It felt like someone was sticking you with a knife, didn't it?
Data.ReadingHint: You learned this reed technique from a group of ninjas who use them to breathe underwater, and you never thought you'd use them like this.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>diarrhea</kanji> is here. It's happening. And it smells so awful you can't breathe. You have to take some hollow <reading>reed</reading>s (り) and shove them up your nose and out the bathroom door so you can get some fresh air in you.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: You ate some <radical>grain</radical>s that made you so <radical>sick</radical> it feels like a <radical>knife</radical> is stabbing in your guts and all your poop is falling out. This is called <kanji>diarrhea</kanji>. Everyone's had diarrhea before, so you should know this feeling by now.

Data.MeaningHint: You pull away the lid and crawl inside. There is a ladder that takes you deep into the pit.
Data.ReadingHint: After you've nursed こういち back to health, he explains that he was put here by a clone こういち–the very same こういち who became emperor. He tells you he can think of around 61-70 different pain levels of revenge, but also that "it probably wouldn't be worth it, so let's just hang out for a while instead. Ooh, are those cookies?"
Data.ReadingMnemonic: When you reach the bottom of the <kanji>pit</kanji>, you hear someone scuffling around and the sounds of chains. You light a match and inch closer to see what it is. In the darkness, you see <reading>こう</reading>いち, starved and chained in a pit under your table.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: In the <radical>dirt</radical>, you find a <radical>lid</radical> under the <radical>table</radical>. You lift it to reveal a deep <kanji>pit</kanji>. Why is there a <kanji>hole</kanji> under your table?

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine if every flower you were supposed to oversee was indigo. Indigo everywhere!
Data.ReadingHint: Can you imagine having indigo-only seeing eyes? I can. Because of this awesome mnemonic.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Wait, maybe these flowers are only <kanji>indigo</kanji> because there is something wrong with your <reading>eye</reading>s (あい)! Maybe your eyes are indigo and that's why it's all you see!
Data.MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Flowers</radical> that you <radical>oversee</radical> are always <kanji>indigo</kanji>. You aren't sure why though...

Data.MeaningHint: Picture a rice paddy. Half of it is trailing off into the shore. Hear the seagulls. Feel the ocean breeze!
Data.ReadingHint: Imagine lying on the shore with Han Solo. Enjoying a cool drink. Smelling the fragrance of the rice paddy behind you. Yeah, this is the life!
Data.ReadingMnemonic: You decide to walk along this <kanji>shore</kanji> when you see <reading>Han</reading> (はん) Solo lounging in the sand. He's waving for you to join him! You get to lie on the shore with Han!!
Data.MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Half</radical> of a <radical>rice paddy</radical> is always on the <kanji>shore</kanji>. Japan is surrounded by water, after all. So it only makes sense that half of every rice paddy is right next to the ocean shore, right?

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine using your mouth to sing and a shell to play the shamisen to perform your shamisen song!
Data.ReadingHint: A shamisen song is a song, yay!
Data.ReadingMnemonic: A <kanji>shamisen song</kanji> is just an <reading>歌</reading> (うた) but with a shamisen. They're still songs so they're said the exact same way!
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Use your <radical>mouth</radical> to sing and a <radical>shellfish</radical> to stroke your shamisen, and you'll be performing a <kanji>shamisen song</kanji> just like that! Yes, it's that easy. Just go find a shell, open your mouth, and you'll be singing one too in no time!

Data.MeaningHint: First, you put them in a coffin to make them think they're going to die. Then you tickle them with your fingers. There is no greater psychological + physical torture than this.
Data.ReadingHint: He keeps screaming: "STOP THIS TICKLE TORTURE. I CAN'T TAKE IT, JUST BURY ME."
Data.ReadingMnemonic: The beggar you will coffin tickle <kanji>torture</kanji> is none other than old Grandpa <reading>ごう</reading>いち. He is a frail old man, and doesn't like to be tickled.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: You put your <radical>fingers</radical> in the <radical>coffin</radical> to tickle the <radical>beggar</radical> inside. What you're doing is a new form of <kanji>torture</kanji>.

Data.MeaningHint: Watch as the tsunami rakes your husband's cleat across the mountain, carving a valley and an easier path for itself. Nature is brutal.
Data.ReadingHint: Imagine yourself eating cake with your husband in this newly formed valley. You both agree that this should be called Cake Valley!
Data.ReadingMnemonic: When the water clears, you and your husband go to the <kanji>valley</kanji> to look for the cleat and instead you find a bunch of <reading>ca</reading>ke (けい)! Maybe this is the tsunami's way of saying, "Thanks for letting me steal your cleat so I could make this valley shortcut."
Data.MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>tsunami</radical> swept away one of the <radical>cleat</radical>s your <radical>husband</radical> just bought and creates an entire <kanji>valley</kanji> with it.

Data.MeaningHint: Look really closely at this Mona Lisa. Hmm, I don't remember her having a mustache, but I guess it is a little dark under this canopy.
Data.ReadingHint: No wonder this moustached Mona Lisa is such a bargain. You can really get anything as a rental these days, huh?
Data.ReadingMnemonic: This Mona Lisa is such a <kanji>bargain</kanji> because it's just a <reading>ren</reading>tal (れん)!
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Under this <radical>canopy</radical> is the <radical>Mona Lisa</radical>, being sold for cheap, what a great <kanji>bargain</kanji>!

Data.MeaningHint: Picture yourself trying to be humble. You're down on the ground talking to a pair of boots. You don't dare raise your eyes past that.
Data.ReadingHint: Imagine saying something to the King. Get low down on the ground to be as humble as you can. Whisper into the King's boots. Lick them if you have to.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: You're being <kanji>humble</kanji> like this because those boots belong to the <reading>Kin</reading>g (きん) of England. You can't just say whatever you want at eye level to a king. He'll have your head chopped off for insolence, or worse!
Data.MeaningMnemonic: You <radical>say</radical> something to a <radical>boot</radical> when you want to be <kanji>humble</kanji>. You're down there because you're bowing in front of the person you're humbling yourself before. Because of that, your face is really close to their boots and you're speaking directly at them, instead of at the person's face, so it's more <kanji>discreet</kanji>.

Data.MeaningHint: Picture the drunk demon as its pretty face drips off revealing the ugly crap piled underneath.
Data.ReadingHint: The demon drinking alcohol out of its nasty shoes is the ugliest scene you've ever seen.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>ugly</kanji> demon is drunk and taking off his <reading>shoe</reading>s (しゅう) and swinging them around his head. And, oh no, he's drinking more alcohol out of the shoes.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Give <radical>alcohol</radical> to a <radical>demon</radical> and you'll see how <kanji>ugly</kanji> it really is. Demons need to concentrate to keep their disguises going. Even if it looked pretty when you met it, like super handsome, give it a few drinks and its facade will slide off and reveal its super gross demon face.

Data.MeaningHint: Picture twenty slides laid out in a grid system.
Data.ReadingHint: You can make a grid out of anything if you set your mind to it. And you can do math anywhere as long as you have some grid paper!
Data.ReadingMnemonic: As you stare at the slide <kanji>grid</kanji> you see some people come out and overhear them talk about their mass <reading>maths</reading> (ます) tournament happening later today. Oohhh it IS a giant grid. And they're going to use it to do maths later!
Data.MeaningMnemonic: You see <radical>twenty</radical> <radical>slide</radical>s lined up like a <kanji>grid</kanji>. You don't know who put them here, but they almost look like the grid paper you do your maths homework in. But who would use a slide as grid paper?

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine all the yakuza coming out into the streets and holding up signs saying this guy was a martyr. A hero to all who do bad stuff in bad groups but don't wanna get caught.
Data.ReadingHint: Really, it's not the fault of the people who put him there that he's become a martyr. It's the month of June. PUNISH JUNE! PUT JUNE IN THE SUN PRISON! (Is what I assume those yakuza are shouting.)
Data.ReadingMnemonic: The only reason he ended up a <kanji>martyr</kanji> in the first place is because they put him up there in <reading>June</reading> (じゅん). Everyone knows the sun is way too hot for humans in June. It's summer!
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Put a <radical>yakuza</radical> member in a <radical>prison</radical> on the <radical>sun</radical> and he'll become a <kanji>martyr</kanji> to all the other yakuza still on earth. He died up there so the rest of them wouldn't have to. Because they definitely aren't getting caught now, if the penalty is a burning death in the new sun prison.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine how annoyed you would be if someone set YOU on fire for no reason. The geoduck has every reason to find you annoying, dude.
Data.ReadingHint: Think how annoying it must be to be a geoduck when Han Solo comes around.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: You know who loves <kanji>annoying</kanji> geoducks more than anyone else in the universe? <reading>Han</reading> (はん) Solo! If you watch Star Wars closely enough you can see him shooting geoducks with his blaster and catching them on fire for fun.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Set <radical>fire</radical> to a <radical>geoduck</radical> if you want to <kanji>annoy</kanji> it. Fire won't kill a geoduck, but have you ever been on fire? It's not really pleasant. So you'll just annoy it pretty badly.

Data.MeaningHint: Picture yourself as you slice the power out of that senator you hate by censuring them in front of the world!
Data.ReadingHint: Look at that guy now. What a loser.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Once you <kanji>censure</kanji> them and they lose all their power, they'll be just another <reading>guy</reading> (がい). A normal guy with everything lost to him. HAHAHA!
Data.MeaningMnemonic: If you want to <radical>slice</radical> the <radical>power</radical> out of someone just <kanji>censure</kanji> them. Then they'll be cut down in front of everyone and they'll feel super embarrassed. It's the best way to slice them down without actually cutting them and getting arrested.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine laying your tree into the water to make your own jetty extending out to the floating bar in the ocean. Innovative!
Data.ReadingHint: Picture Santa-san coming in from the ocean on his speedboat and docking on your jetty so he can go relax in the bar. Imagine the mountain of presents he lays on the jetty for you as thanks.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: You also made this <kanji>jetty</kanji> so it would be easier for <reading>San</reading>ta-san (さん) to dock his sleigh. Oh, you didn't know? His sleigh is more of a speed boat now, and he wants a refreshing drink after delivering all those presents. You get extra presents for making him a jetty, so win-win.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: You grab a <radical>tree</radical> to help you reach the <radical>bar</radical> out in the ocean, using it as a <kanji>jetty</kanji> leading straight to it. Now you can walk out on your makeshift <kanji>pier</kanji> and go get drinks without needing a boat or getting wet.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine what it would be like to fall in love with a coat rack and make it YOUR groom.
Data.ReadingHint: Try to imagine what this moon coat looks like. Does it glow in the dark like the moon shines at night? Or does it instead have a rugged texture like the moon's surface? Or maybe both?
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Since their wedding happened under the full moon, the <kanji>groom</kanji> wore his special <reading>moon coa</reading>t (むこ) to the ceremony. This is the ideal coat for a groom to wear, since they can keep wearing it all through their honeymoon.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Have you heard the tale of the <radical>woman</radical> who fell in love with a <radical>coat rack</radical> during a full <radical>moon</radical> and made him her <kanji>groom</kanji>? It's a true story! She even had a wedding ceremony and had more coat racks sitting in the groom's side. The moon will make people do craaaazy things.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine how it feels to yearn for someone else when you see the small water drop. Imagine how your heart feels.
Data.ReadingHint: Now you yearn for being somewhere Bobo is not.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: As you <kanji>yearn for</kanji> that person from your past, you look back at the drop and see... <reading>Bo</reading>bo (ぼ) the clown?! Ugh. Apparently Bobo dropped that small drop of water.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: You're walking in a <radical>greenhouse</radical> when you see a <radical>small</radical> <radical>drop</radical> of water fall and it makes you <kanji>yearn for</kanji> the person you <kanji>adore</kanji>. You haven't seen them in so long. But something about being in this greenhouse and seeing that tiny water droplet made your heart burst with longing.

Data.MeaningHint: Your captors think they have you trapped, but you know you can leave whenever you feel like it.
Data.ReadingHint: As the heat spreads you decide to leave. You hop out of the net and watch the shock spread in your captors eyes.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: You don't know if you want to <kanji>quit</kanji> this charade until you feel an uncomfortable <reading>hea</reading>t (ひ). You look down and the net you're in is being hung above a large fire.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: You're in a <radical>net</radical> but you have the <radical>ability</radical> to <kanji>quit</kanji> playing along and <kanji>leave</kanji> at any time.

Data.MeaningHint: An arrow from heaven will correct all your problems!
Data.ReadingHint: If you want someone to correct your teeth or mustache, just wander around きょうと, staring up at the sky. Then when you see an arrow, run for it and try to catch it with your face!
Data.ReadingMnemonic: These heavenly <kanji>correct</kanji>ing arrows actually come from <reading>きょう</reading>と. They hate seeing people with crooked mustaches, so they shoot them out on the regular. It just looks like they're coming from heaven because they fall out of the sky.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: An <radical>arrow </radical>is shot from <radical>heaven</radical> and hits right between your <radical>mouth</radical> and your <radical>mustache</radical> to <kanji>correct</kanji> your crooked smile. It also <kanji>straighten</kanji>s your teeth and the crooked cut of your mustache too. Who told you that looked good? Well, it doesn't matter. The heaven arrow corrected it for you.

Data.MeaningHint: Which tree do you think is sweet? That one? Or that one? Are you absolutely certain? I hope you are!
Data.ReadingHint: So now let me ask you again, are you certain, or would you like to try the bowling ball test?
Data.ReadingMnemonic: If you really want to be <kanji>certain</kanji>, and not just guess, take out your <reading>bow</reading>ling (ぼう) ball and roll it as hard as you can into the tree. If its the right one it will let out a sweet smell, if it's not the tree will punch you.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: If you think a <radical>tree</radical> looks <radical>sweet</radical> you have to be <kanji>certain</kanji> before you bite into it. Trees are really hard, and usually pretty bitter (believe me) so if you don't want a mouthful of broken teeth, sap, and sadness, you really need to be sure it's the one before you start munching.

Data.MeaningHint: Certain tsunami secretions are dangerous. Can you think of any?
Data.ReadingHint: The next time someone asks you what does a tsunami secrete? You'll say heat!
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Most tsunami's <kanji>secrete</kanji> <reading>he</reading>at (ひ). It takes a lot of energy for a tsunami to crash onto the land so the water is really really hot, and it has to secrete that heat as it's going back out to sea.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Tsunami</radical>'s have a <radical>certain</radical> tendency to <kanji>secrete</kanji> things as they go back out to sea. When they bash themselves onto the land they just throw a bunch of sea water around, but there are only certain things that they'll secrete when they're leaving.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine gradually denting your car door open with an axe while you're trapped in a tsunami. This isn't fast enough!
Data.ReadingHint: YO YANKEES! Gradually yelling that saved your life!
Data.ReadingMnemonic: As you're <kanji>gradually</kanji> making your door open with the axe you look up to see the New York Yankees floating by on top of their bus. You yell, "<reading>YO YA</reading>NKEES (ようや) to get their attention. They see your gradual progress and start throwing balls at your car to help dent it open.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: You're stuck in your <radical>car</radical> during a <radical>tsunami</radical> and all you have to break out is an <radical>axe</radical>. You whack and whack but you're only <kanji>gradually</kanji> making progress. You thought an axe would be an easy way to get out of a car but it's not working like you'd planned! You have to make more than gradual progress if you don't want to drown.

Data.MeaningHint: Seaweed is the flower of the sea!
Data.ReadingHint: Feed your soul with seaweed and it will thank you. Promise! <3
Data.ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>Seaweed</kanji> is true <reading>sou</reading>l (そう) food. Which means it's good for your soul. Didn't you ever read those books about chicken soup being good for your soul? Well that was a lie. Miso soup with seaweed is what's really good for your soul.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>flowers</radical> that wash up on shore in <radical>tsunami</radical>s that we make into <radical>syrup</radical> is <kanji>seaweed</kanji>. Okay, so it's more like soup or tea than syrup, but you can make them both out of seaweed.

Data.MeaningHint: Picture yourself watching the news about a woman's death. The word RECKLESS?! pops up on your screen and keeps flashing. Huh, she must have been, you think to yourself.
Data.ReadingHint: Imagine what reckless thing that woman had to have done to die while mowing the lawn. Eww.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: As you watch the news about this <kanji>reckless</kanji> woman you find out that she died from <reading>mow</reading>ing (もう) her lawn. You're not sure how, but now you really believe that she had to be doing it really recklessly to die doing it.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: If the <radical>death</radical> of a <radical>woman</radical> occurs it's because she was being <kanji>reckless</kanji>. People don't just die out of nowhere! They have to have done something super crazy to cause it. Right? Yes... right.

Data.MeaningHint: Again and again, these insect-like barbarians are harassing you and your lands. What shall we do, m'lord?
Data.ReadingHint: Picture yourself as the lord of your lands, screaming "BAN them" at the top of your lungs. You'll finally expel these insect-like barbarians that have been plaguing your lands again and again.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: "<reading>BAN</reading> (ばん) the barbarians! Ban them, I say!," you loudly declare as the lord of these lands.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: If, <radical>again</radical> and again, you behave like an <radical>insect</radical>, you might as well be a <kanji>barbarian</kanji>.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine the leader squid giving a speech about how you shouldn't buy new things, and how you should live with a roommate to save money. Every squid yen counts!
Data.ReadingHint:
Ken's squid is always shaking his head at Ken the samurai's obsession with wearing all silk. He should visit Thrifty Threads, the second hand shop, and get some cotton clothes that would do the job just fine.

Hint: Whenever there's a squid, remember that Ken (けん) the samurai is nearby.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Who is a <kanji>thrifty</kanji> squid? <reading>Ken</reading> (けん) the samurai's squid buddy, who gets carried around in a bucket and never pays rent.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> <radical>squid</radical> tells all of its squid people to be <kanji>thrifty</kanji>.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine the heat of the sun on your forehead as you watch these horrible creatures with lid-heads and fin-feet. There's no doubt they're from the underworld, probably here to enjoy the sun. You might want to get out of their way…
Data.ReadingHint: Yes, even underworld creatures love to frolic around in the beautiful May weather.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: These <kanji>underworld</kanji> creatures must be out and about because it's <reading>May</reading> (めい), and the weather is so fantastic and sunny right now. They don't get to see the sun down there in the underworld, so every May they turn up in droves to experience it.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: You go outside to bask your <radical>forehead</radical> in the <radical>sun</radical>, but then you see these creatures with <radical>lid</radical>s for heads and <radical>fins</radical> for feet. Uh… where did they come from? From the <kanji>underworld</kanji>. Nothing like them exists in this world. They obviously came from the underworld.
